name: Mayahuel
description: Mayahuel is the Aztec goddess of the maguey plant, and by extension, pulque, an alcoholic beverage derived from it. She is a fertility goddess associated with nourishment, abundance, and drunkenness. Often depicted with numerous breasts, symbolizing her role as a provider, Mayahuel is considered a vital figure in Aztec agricultural and social life. Her origins are rooted in the earth and its bounty, and she is sometimes considered an aspect of other mother goddesses. Legend states she was a beautiful mortal woman, hidden away by her grandmother, Tzitzimitl, to prevent her from marrying Quetzalcoatl. Quetzalcoatl, in the form of a tree branch, convinced her to flee with him. Tzitzimitl, upon discovering their escape, killed Mayahuel, leaving Quetzalcoatl heartbroken. From her remains sprung the first maguey plant, a testament to her sacrifice and enduring life-giving power. Pulque, born from the maguey, is regarded as a sacred drink, used in rituals and ceremonies, connecting humans to the divine. Her role extended beyond mere sustenance; she represented the intoxicating joy of life and the spiritual connection facilitated through ritual and communal celebration. Mayahuel's story underscores themes of sacrifice, transformation, and the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth. She embodies the power of the feminine, the earth's generosity, and the transformative potential of both nourishment and intoxication.
subtitle: Goddess of Maguey and Pulque

keyMyths.title: Mayahuel's Escape with Quetzalcoatl
keyMyths.description: Mayahuel, hidden by her grandmother, escapes with Quetzalcoatl in the form of a tree branch. Tzitzimitl discovers their betrayal and murders Mayahuel.

keyMyths.title: The Origin of the Maguey Plant
keyMyths.description: From Mayahuel's remains, Quetzalcoatl buries her bones, and the first maguey plant sprouts, symbolizing her life-giving essence.

keyMyths.title: Pulque as a Sacred Drink
keyMyths.description: Pulque, derived from the maguey, becomes a sacred drink used in Aztec rituals and ceremonies, connecting humans with the divine realm.

worshipPractices.name: Pulque Offerings
worshipPractices.description: Pulque, the fermented beverage derived from the maguey plant, was a primary offering to Mayahuel. This offering symbolized gratitude for her provision and sought her continued blessings of fertility and abundance. The ritual consumption of pulque also played a role in connecting with the divine.

worshipPractices.name: Maguey Planting Rituals
worshipPractices.description: Rituals surrounding the planting and harvesting of maguey were performed to honor Mayahuel and ensure a successful yield. These rituals often involved prayers, dances, and offerings to the goddess, acknowledging her role in the life cycle of the plant.

festivals.name: Tecuilhuitontli
festivals.timing: The eighth month of the Aztec calendar
festivals.description: Tecuilhuitontli, meaning 'the feast of the little lords', was dedicated to several deities associated with fertility and sustenance, including Mayahuel. During this festival, offerings were made to ensure a bountiful harvest and the continued prosperity of the community. Dances and feasts were held in her honor.

cultural.modernLegacy: While formal worship of Mayahuel has ceased, her symbolism persists in Mexican culture. The maguey plant remains a vital agricultural resource, and pulque continues to be produced and consumed. Mayahuel is remembered as a symbol of fertility, nourishment, and the life-sustaining power of the earth. She is sometimes invoked in art and cultural celebrations related to traditional agriculture.

extendedContent.title: Origins & Birth
extendedContent.content: The origins of Mayahuel are intertwined with the very essence of the maguey plant, a staple of Aztec life. According to legend, Mayahuel was a beautiful maiden, often described as a goddess, who was kept hidden away by her grandmother, Tzitzimitl, a fearsome star demon. Quetzalcoatl, the feathered serpent god, discovered her and, enamored by her beauty and imprisoned state, convinced her to flee with him to Earth. To escape Tzitzimitl's wrath, they transformed themselves into a tree with two branches. However, Tzitzimitl discovered their hiding place and, enraged, tore the tree apart, killing Mayahuel. Quetzalcoatl gathered her remains and buried them, from which the first maguey plant sprung forth, forever linking Mayahuel to this vital resource. Her birth is thus a tale of sacrifice and the transformation of divine essence into earthly sustenance.

extendedContent.title: Major Myths & Stories
extendedContent.content: The most prominent myth surrounding Mayahuel is her escape with Quetzalcoatl and subsequent transformation into the maguey plant. This story highlights her role as a sacrifice for the benefit of humanity. The maguey plant, born from her remains, provides not only pulque but also fibers for clothing, materials for construction, and medicinal properties. Another story tells of Mayahuel having four hundred rabbit children, the Centzon Totochtin, who represent the different states of drunkenness induced by pulque. These rabbit gods embody the various effects of the beverage, from merriment to disorientation. This association further solidifies Mayahuel's connection to pulque and its complex role in Aztec society, where it was both a sacred drink and a source of potential chaos.

extendedContent.title: Worship & Cult
extendedContent.content: Mayahuel was venerated as a vital deity in Aztec agricultural and social life. Her worship centered around the maguey plant and its products, particularly pulque. Offerings of pulque were made to her during festivals and rituals, seeking her blessings for fertility, abundance, and a successful harvest. Priests and priestesses oversaw these ceremonies, ensuring the proper protocols were followed to appease the goddess and maintain the balance between the human and divine realms. The consumption of pulque itself was a ritual act, believed to facilitate communication with the gods and provide spiritual insight. Mayahuel's temples, though perhaps not as grand as those dedicated to major deities like Huitzilopochtli or Tlaloc, were important centers for agricultural rituals and community gatherings.

extendedContent.title: Legacy & Influence
extendedContent.content: Although the formal worship of Mayahuel declined after the Spanish conquest, her influence continues to resonate in Mexican culture. The maguey plant remains a significant agricultural resource, and pulque is still produced and consumed in some regions. Mayahuel's image, often depicted with numerous breasts, symbolizing her role as a provider, persists in art and folklore. She represents the life-giving power of the earth and the importance of agriculture in sustaining human civilization. In modern times, there is a renewed interest in pre-Columbian cultures, and Mayahuel is being rediscovered as a symbol of indigenous heritage and the enduring connection between humanity and the natural world. Her story serves as a reminder of the rich mythology and traditions that shaped Aztec society.

shortDescription: Mayahuel is the Aztec goddess of the maguey plant and pulque. She embodies fertility, nourishment, and abundance in Aztec mythology.
